envelopes.hydrated_text:
The 2025 Atlantic hurricane season produced 19 named storms, 11 hurricanes, and 6 major hurricanes — above the 1991-2020 climatology of 14/7/3. Accumulated cyclone energy (ACE) was 168, the third-highest in the satellite era after 2017 and 2020.

The season was characterised by an unusually active early phase (three hurricanes by July 15) and a pronounced gap from late August through mid-September normally associated with peak activity. This gap is attributable to a persistent Saharan Air Layer outbreak that suppressed convection across the main development region for nearly six weeks. We discuss the implications for medium-range hurricane forecasting, which currently does not skilfully predict SAL outbreaks at the 4-6 week lead time that would be most actionable.

Three storms reached category 5 strength (Helene, Lorenzo, and Marina), tying the 2005 record. Rapid intensification — defined as a 30-knot increase in 24 hours — occurred in 41% of named storms, well above the historical 20% rate, consistent with continued ocean-heat-content increases in the main development region.

Tropical cyclone rapid intensification (RI) frequency has increased significantly across all major basins over the satellite era. We use a high-resolution coupled-model ensemble to disentangle the drivers and find that increased ocean heat content alone explains roughly 60% of the observed RI increase, with reduced wind shear contributing an additional 25% and improved coupling between deep convection and surface fluxes contributing the remaining 15%.

Projections through 2100 under a moderate-emissions scenario suggest RI frequency will continue to rise at roughly 1.2% per decade, but with substantial regional variation: the Eastern Pacific shows the largest projected increase, while the Western Pacific shows a more modest signal due to projected increases in vertical wind shear that partially offset thermodynamic favourability.

Forecasting RI remains a major operational challenge, with current 24-hour skill scores below 0.4. We argue that machine-learning methods trained on the now-substantial satellite microwave record offer the most promising near-term path to improvement, with several published systems already approaching skill scores of 0.5.

ENSO conditions in April 2026 are weak La Nina, with NINO 3.4 anomalies of -0.7 C. This is the third consecutive winter with La Nina or borderline conditions, an unusual triple-dip pattern previously observed only in 1973-1976, 1998-2001, and 2020-2023.

The Climate Prediction Center forecast issued today places 60% probability on a transition to ENSO-neutral by July-September 2026, with a 25% probability of weak El Nino developing by year-end and 15% probability of continued La Nina. Models are unusually divergent at this lead time, with the dynamical-model consensus favouring transition while statistical models favour persistence.

For seasonal forecasts, the practical implication is that confidence in temperature and precipitation outlooks for the 2026 summer is below average. We recommend users of seasonal outlooks reference the probabilistic forecasts directly rather than rely on the deterministic 'most likely' category, given the elevated model spread.

bullets: The 2025 Atlantic hurricane season produced 19 named storms (above the 14-storm climatology) with the third-highest ACE in the satellite era, including three category-5 storms and a 41% rapid-intensification rate well above the historical 20%. (env-08-a)

Tropical cyclone rapid intensification has increased across all basins primarily due to ocean heat content changes (~60% of signal), with projections suggesting continued ~1.2% per decade increase under moderate emissions. (env-08-b)

ENSO conditions are entering a third consecutive La Nina winter, an unusual triple-dip pattern, with model consensus split between transition-to-neutral and persistence and consequently below-average confidence in 2026 seasonal outlooks. (env-08-c)

notes: tight, comfortable cluster: 1.5h span, 0.82 cosine; topical thread is Atlantic tropical climate (hurricane season + RI + ENSO)
